1: A type: 'Out of Egypt I called my son.' The Hosea 11:1 citation in Matthew 2:15 reveals the NT's typological method: the Exodus — YHWH calling Israel his son out of Egypt — is recapitulated in miniature by the holy family's flight to Egypt and return. Jesus is the true Son of God where Israel was the representative son; Jesus is the true Israel who succeeds where Israel repeatedly failed. The NT development of this recapitulation: Israel spent 40 years in the wilderness and failed (Num 14); Jesus spent 40 days in the wilderness and prevailed (Matt 4). Israel crossed the Jordan under Joshua and conquered imperfectly; Jesus was baptized in the Jordan and accomplished the complete conquest of sin and death. The Exodus story is not just a historical event that Jesus parallels — it is the template YHWH designed to interpret what his Son would do.
2: Type — 'The more they were called, the more they went away' — the prodigal pattern of Israel: divine call producing only greater departure. Luke 15:11-24: the prodigal son goes further away while the father waits. Jesus tells this parable as the definitive account of the Israel dynamic — the son who goes away is Israel; the waiting, running father is YHWH. The parable is Hosea 11 in narrative form.
3: Type — 'I taught Ephraim to walk, taking him by the arms; but they did not know that I healed them' — parental care unrecognized. John 9:1-41: Jesus heals the man born blind, who does not initially know who healed him. The pattern is exact: divine healing administered, recipient unaware of the healer. Isa 53:4: 'Surely he has borne our sicknesses… yet we esteemed him stricken by God.' The misidentified healer is Christ.
4: Direct — 'I led them with cords of human kindness, with ropes of love. I was like someone who lifts the yoke from their neck and bends down to feed them' — the three images (cords of love, lifted yoke, feeding from below) map directly onto Christ's actions. John 12:32: 'I, when I am lifted up, will draw all people to myself' — drawing by love-cords. Matt 11:29: 'my yoke is easy' — the yoke lifted. John 13:5: Jesus bends down to wash feet — the posture of the Father bending to feed becomes the Son bending to serve.
5: Theme — 'He will not return to Egypt; the Assyrian will be his king, because they refused to repent' — exile as the consequence of refusal to turn. Luke 13:3: 'Unless you repent, you will all likewise perish.' Jesus' call is the final, fullest extension of YHWH's call through Hosea. The exile Hosea announces for refusal of repentance is the framework Jesus warns against in national and individual terms.
6: Type — Sword consuming strongholds because of their schemes — the fall of Jerusalem's defenses as divine judgment on self-reliance. Matt 24:2: 'Not one stone here will be left on another; every one will be thrown down.' Jesus explicitly predicts the fall of the fortified temple-city in the same theological pattern: self-trusting strength demolished by divine judgment.
7: Type — 'My people are determined to turn away from me' — the determined apostasy of a people committed to departure. John 6:66-68: 'From this time many of his disciples turned back and no longer walked with him.' Jesus asks the Twelve: 'Do you want to go away as well?' The determination to leave is exactly Hosea's 'determined to turn away.' Peter's answer ('Lord, to whom shall we go?') is the right response Hosea's Israel never gave.
8: Direct — 'How can I give you up, Ephraim? How can I hand you over, Israel?' — divine anguish at impending judgment; the Father's heart resisting what justice requires. Matt 23:37: 'Jerusalem, Jerusalem… how often I wanted to gather your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, and you were not willing!' Jesus voices the exact same divine anguish, now in the first person, standing over the city. The 'How can I give you up?' of Hosea becomes the 'How often I wanted!' of Jesus.
9: Revelation of God — 'I am God and not a human being — the Holy One in your midst — and I will not come to destroy' — the divine nature as the ground of unexpected mercy. Phil 2:6-8: 'though he was in the form of God… he emptied himself, taking the form of a servant, being made in human likeness.' The Incarnation reverses the Hosea statement not by contradiction but by fulfillment: the God-not-human becomes human precisely to save rather than destroy. The Holy One's presence 'in your midst' becomes Emmanuel — God with us — in human form.
10: Direct — 'He will roar like a lion. When he roars, his children will come trembling from the west' — Rev 5:5: 'The Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered.' The lion roar of YHWH that gathers the scattered finds its ultimate agent in Christ identified as the Lion. The children who 'come trembling' from east and west are the eschatological ingathering: Matt 8:11 ('many will come from east and west and recline at table with Abraham').
11: Theme — 'They will come trembling like birds from Egypt and like doves from Assyria' — the full international ingathering of scattered Israel. John 11:52: Christ 'would die… to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad.' The scope of the gathering expands in the NT beyond ethnic Israel to all who are God's children, bringing in those from every nation in fulfillment of the pattern Hosea announces.
12: Theme — Ephraim surrounded by lies, Judah faithful to the Holy One — the contrast between deceptive speech and truth. John 14:6: 'I am the way, the truth, and the life.' John 8:44: the devil is the father of lies, and Ephraim's surrounding YHWH with lies reflects this. Against the lies that surround God, Christ comes as the truth itself — the one who cannot lie and in whom there is no deception.
